title: 3 1 Vector algebra
body:
TOPIC 3 : VECTOR FIELDS
                             3.1 VECTOR ALGEBRA

                                          Given vectors

        u = u1 i + u2 j + u3 k,       v = v1 i + v2 j + v3 k,   w = w1 i + w2 j + w3 k

                         we can define the following operations


    u · v = u1 v1 + u2 v2 + u3 v3 .                    dot product : vector to scalar

                 i j k
    u×v =       u1 u2 u3                          cross product : vector to vector
                v1 v2 v3
                     u1 u2 u3
    u · (v × w) =    v1 v2 v3                      triple product : vector to scalar
                     w1 w2 w3


We begin this topic with a quick review of first year vector algebra and kinematics.

Fact: That the dot product of two orthogonal vectors is zero.
                                                     
                         2                              1
Example 1 Let v = −1 = 2i − j + 3k and w = 8 = i + 8j + 2k. Use the dot
                         3                              2
product to prove that v and w are orthogonal.




                                                   1
 Fact: The cross product of two vectors creates a new vector in the orthogonal direction.

Example 2 Let u = i + 2j + 4k and v = 3i + 5j + k. Find the cross product u × v.

Check your answer with the dot product.




Fact: |u × v| is the area of the parallelogram with sides u and v.

Fact: |u · (v × w)| is the volume of the parallelepiped with sides u, v, and w.

Example 3 Find the volume of the parallelepiped with one corner at P and sides
P Q, P R and P S where P, Q, R, S are: P (3, 1, 1), Q(0, 1, 6), R(5, 5, 7), S(2, 1, 6).

Note that we have two different ways of evaluating the scalar triple product.




                                           2
     F 40 F

3
                                         Kinematics
Vectors can be used to describe the path of a particle travelling through space. We have
already seen a simple example of this with the parametric equation for a straight line:

                          x(t) = x0 + b1 t
                          y(t) = y0 + b2 t    −→     r(t) = r0 + bt.
                          z(t) = z0 + b3 t

Here, r(t) = x(t)i + y(t)j + z(t)k is the position of the particle at some time t, r0 =
x0 i + y0 j + z0 k is the initial position at time t = 0, and b = b1 i + b2 j + b3 k is the unit
vector pointing in the direction of motion.

More generally, we can use the time-dependent position vector r(t) to represent any
parametric path through space. The velocity and acceleration of the particle’s motion
along the path are given by the time derivatives of the position,

                                 r(t) = x(t)i + y(t)j + z(t)k

                                  dr
                              v(t) =  = ẋ(t)i + ẏ(t)j + ż(t)k
                                  dt
                               dv    d2 r
                        a(t) =    = 2 = ẍ(t)i + ÿ(t)j + z̈(t)k
                               dt    dt
Note that we have introduced the dot notation to indicate derivative with respect to time.

The magnitude of the velocity (a vector) gives us the speed (a scalar) of the particle along
the path. The velocity vector is always tangential to the path. We can see this by looking
at the change in position of the particle along the path from time t and time t + ∆t,

                                  ∆r(t) = r(t + ∆t) − r(t)




The vector ∆r(t) points along the path travelled by the particle and will be tangential to
the path as ∆t becomes very small. But the velocity vector is simply the time derivative
of the position,
                                             r(t + ∆t) − r(t)
                               v(t) = lim                     .
                                        ∆t→0       ∆t
So the velocity vector is in the same direction as ∆r in the limit of very small times
∆t, i.e. the velocity is tangential to the path. The acceleration vector depends upon the
curvature of the path and can point in any direction.

                                               4
 Example 4 Consider the path in R2 given by r(t) = cos(t)i + sin(t)j

Show that the path is a circle and prove that the velocity vector is always tangential to
the circle and the acceleration of the particle is directed to the centre of the circle. (This
is always the case for circular motion.)




Example 5 Consider the path in R2 given by r(t) = cos(3t)i + sin(3t)j. How is this
motion different from the previous example?




                                              5
 Example 6 Suppose that a particle moves through space along the path

                               r(t) = cos(t)i + sin(t)j + tk.

Describe the path and determine the speed and acceleration of the particle after π4 seconds.




                                                                √
                                                 F speed =          2, acceleration = 1 F

                                             6
 In a previous lecture we have seen that the gradient ∇φ at a point P always points per-
pendicular to the level surface at that point. We can now prove this result.

Proof: Let φ(x, y, z) be a scalar field with a level surface φ(x, y, z) = c. Let x = x(t),
y = y(t) and z = z(t) be any parametrically defined path on the surface φ(x, y, z) = c.




Differentiating φ(x, y, z) = c with respect to t and using the chain rule gives

                               ∂φ dx ∂φ dy ∂φ dz
                                     +       +       = 0.
                               ∂x dt   ∂y dt   ∂z dt
Using the definition of the dot product, this can be written as

                                         ∇φ · v = 0,

where
                                         dx   dy dz
                                  v=        i+ j+ k
                                         dt   dt dt
is the velocity vector along the path.

Now, the velocity vector is always at a tangent to the path. Since ∇φ · v = 0 for any
path, this means that the gradient ∇φ is perpendicular to all tangent vectors on the level
surface. Thus, the gradient is perpendicular to the level surface everywhere.

We will now demonstrate the proof with an example.


                                             7
 Example 7 Consider the scalar field T (x, y, z) = x2 +y 2 +z 2 . Let x(t) = 5 cos t, y(t) = 0,
z(t) = 5 sin t be a parametrically defined path on the level surface T (x, y, z) = 25 passing
through the point P (3, 0, 4). Show that the velocity vector along the path is perpendicular
to the gradient at the point P (3, 0, 4).




                                                                                         F




Figure 1: Example 8: The gradient vector (in red) is perpendicular to the velocity vectors
(in pink) along the path x = 5 cos t, y = 0, z = 5 sin t on the surface T (x, y, z) = 25.
